stories.title: Jose Ramirez First Guardian Ever to HR vs. All 29 Teams

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Jose Ramirez just made history.

He hit a home run off Martin Perez on Friday — becoming the first player in Guardians franchise history to homer against all 29 other MLB teams.

125 years of franchise history. No one else had ever done it. 🔥

#GoGuardians #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
The Guardians won 6-0 Friday behind Jose Ramirez (HR in 1st off Perez) and Parker Messick (6.2 IP, 0 ER vs. Atlanta).

For Ramirez: the franchise HR record. The games-played record. Now the 29-team HR club.

He's writing his Hall of Fame case in real time. 🔥

#GoGuardians #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 9:30 AM ET*

In 125 years of Cleveland baseball — through the Naps era, the Indians era, and the Guardians era — no player had ever homered against all 29 opponents. Jose Ramirez took care of that Friday night.

His first-inning shot off Atlanta's Martin Perez completed the set. Every team. Every opponent in Major League Baseball. The franchise's best player delivered a milestone that no one — not Larry Doby, not Jim Thome, not Manny Ramirez — had achieved before him.

Cleveland won 6-0. Parker Messick was brilliant on the mound (6.2 innings, 0 runs). But the story of the night was Ramirez checking off the last name on a list that had been building for more than a century.

Put this in the context of April 2026 for Ramirez: He broke the franchise record for most games played (1,620 on April 6). He's pacing for AL Player of the Month at .342/.722 SLG with 7 HRs and 28 RBI in 21 games. And now this.

If you're making a Hall of Fame case in 2026, Jose Ramirez has been making it louder every single day.

What do you think — is Ramirez already a Hall of Famer based on what he's built in Cleveland?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with game photo*

First in franchise history. 125 years of Cleveland baseball. Jose Ramirez just became the first Guardian ever to homer against all 29 other MLB teams. 🔥

article.headline: In 125 Years of Cleveland Baseball, No One Had Done This Until Jose Ramirez

stories.title: Dodgers 11-3: Ohtani Leadoff HR, Sheehan Dominates Rangers

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Shohei Ohtani. Leadoff. Home run. First pitch of the first inning.

His 4th HR of 2026 (104.5 mph exit velocity, 390 ft) set the tone as the Dodgers won 6-3 Friday to go 11-3. 🔥

Teoscar Hernandez added a 3-run shot. Emmet Sheehan: 6 IP, 2-0.

#Dodgers #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
The Dodgers are 11-3 — best record in baseball — without Mookie Betts.

Max Muncy 3-HR walk-off Thursday. Ohtani leadoff HR Friday. The lineup just doesn't stop.

Betts said he's "symptom-free" and ahead of schedule. 👀

#Dodgers #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 12:00 PM ET*

Shohei Ohtani hit a leadoff home run on Friday night. 104.5 mph off the bat. 390 feet to right field. Fourth homer of the 2026 season. First pitch of the first inning.

Then Teoscar Hernandez hit a three-run shot immediately after. By the time the first inning ended, the Dodgers were ahead 4-0 and the Rangers were already behind the eight-ball. Emmet Sheehan made it stick with six innings of four-hit ball, improving to 2-0 on the season.

Los Angeles won 6-3 and moved to 11-3 — the best record in baseball. And they've done it without Mookie Betts, who has been on the injured list since April 4 with a right oblique strain. Dave Roberts says Betts is "symptom-free" and expected back before the end of April.

Here's the kicker: The Dodgers have scored 45 runs in their first 4 road games this season. That's the most by any team in their first four road games since 1900.

Two weeks in. Best record in baseball. Ohtani approaching his second pitching start of the season. The Dodgers look absolutely terrifying.

How far do you think this team goes in October?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Ohtani photo*

Ohtani. Leadoff. Home run. Fourth of the season. Dodgers win 6-3, improve to 11-3 — the best record in baseball. Without Mookie Betts. 🔥

stories.title: Red Sox Win First Series of 2026 After Historic 2-8 Start

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
The Boston Red Sox finally won a series.

After a 2-8 start that tied the worst 10-game opening in 126 years of franchise history, the Red Sox beat St. Louis 7-1 on Saturday.

Ranger Suarez: 6 scoreless innings. Willson Contreras: 3 RBIs. 🙌

#RedSox #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Alex Cora called the 2-8 start "unacceptable" last week.

The Red Sox have now won back-to-back games for the first time since September 2025.

It's a small step. But for a team that needed one, it matters. 

#RedSox #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 1:30 PM ET*

It took two weeks and nine losses, but the Boston Red Sox finally won a series.

A 7-1 win over St. Louis on Saturday — powered by Ranger Suárez pitching six scoreless innings and Willson Contreras driving in three runs — gave Boston back-to-back wins for the first time since September 2025. Combined with their earlier series win over Milwaukee (Sonny Gray: 6.1 shutout innings), the Red Sox have escaped their early-season basement in style.

To understand the weight of this moment: Boston's 2-8 start tied the worst 10-game opening in 126 years of franchise history. Alex Cora publicly called it "unacceptable." The team that paid serious money for Suárez, Gray, and Contreras this offseason was getting absolutely nothing from those additions in April.

Now, at least for one weekend, they delivered. Suárez was sharp. Gray was dominant. Contreras drove in runs. That's what the offseason was supposed to look like.

The Red Sox are still 5-8, still among baseball's worst records. But they've given themselves a reason to believe the turnaround is possible.

Is this a real turning point, or a two-game sample in a long season?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with team photo*

Boston Red Sox: first series win of 2026. After a 2-8 start that tied 126 years of franchise misery, Suárez and Contreras finally delivered on Saturday. Small step, but a real one. 🙌

stories.title: Konnor Griffin Debuts, Hits RBI Double, Signs $140M Extension

x_posts.label: Post 4-1
x_posts.text:
Konnor Griffin is 19, was MLB's No. 1 overall prospect, hit an RBI double in his MLB debut — and then signed a 9-year, $140M extension.

The largest contract ever given to a player before their first big league game.

The Pirates might be for real. ⚾

#LetsGoBucs #MLB

x_posts.label: Post 4-2
x_posts.text:
For context on the Konnor Griffin deal:

Before his debut he had played ZERO MLB games. The previous record for pre-debut extensions? $95M for Colt Emerson of the Mariners — set LAST WEEK.

$140M before a single at-bat. Pittsburgh believes. ⚾

#LetsGoBucs #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 3:30 PM ET*

Let's talk about the most remarkable week in Pittsburgh Pirates baseball in years.

Konnor Griffin — 19 years old, the No. 1 overall prospect in baseball, the ninth pick in the 2024 draft — was called up for the Pirates' home opener. In his first MLB at-bat, he hit an RBI double. Then, within days of his debut, Griffin signed a 9-year, $140 million contract extension.

$140 million. For a player who hadn't played a single big league game when negotiations started. That's the largest contract ever given to a pre-debut player. It broke the record set just the week prior — when the Mariners gave Colt Emerson 8 years and $95 million.

The message from Pittsburgh ownership is clear: they believe this kid is a franchise cornerstone. They're not waiting for him to prove it over multiple seasons. They're betting now, before the market figures out what he's worth.

Griffin had posted a .438 average in five Triple-A games before the call-up. At 19, he's playing with the poise of someone years older.

For a Pirates fan base that has endured a lot of rebuilds and false starts, Griffin's arrival — and the contract commitment behind it — feels like something genuinely different.

Do you think $140M was the right move before Griffin had played a major league game?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with Griffin action photo*

MLB's No. 1 prospect. RBI double in his first at-bat. $140M extension before his career began. Konnor Griffin arrived this week — and Pittsburgh is all in. ⚾

stories.title: Yankees Swept by Rays — Three Straight Losses From 8-2 Start

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
The Yankees started 8-2. They've since lost three straight to the Rays.

Ben Rice is at 1.183 OPS. Luis Gil's debut was rough. The bullpen has been shaky.

New York is 8-5 and needs to figure this out fast. 👀

#Yankees #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 5:30 PM ET*

The New York Yankees started 8-2. They had the best ERA in baseball, allowed fewer than two runs per game, and looked like a team that might run away with the American League.

Then the Tampa Bay Rays happened.

Three straight losses at Tropicana Field — 5-3 on Thursday (Luis Gil's uneven debut), 5-3 again on Friday — have dropped the Yankees to 8-5 and raised real questions about whether the early-season dominance was sustainable. Yandy Díaz hit a go-ahead homer in the opener. The Rays' lineup kept finding ways to get to the Yankees bullpen.

The good news in New York: Ben Rice has been electric. His 1.183 OPS, four home runs, and 100th percentile expected wOBA through 11 games suggest he's the real deal, not just a hot streak. He's been the best offensive player on the Yankees roster through two weeks.

The bad news: the rotation is banged up, Luis Gil needs more runway to find his form, and the defense hasn't been sharp in Tampa.

A three-game losing streak in April isn't a crisis. But after the start they had, it's worth paying attention.

Are you still concerned about the Yankees, or is this just an April blip?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with game photo*

Yankees swept. Three straight losses to Tampa Bay. From 8-2 to 8-5. The hot start has stalled — but Ben Rice (1.183 OPS, 4 HR) is still making noise. 👀

stories.title: NL Central After Week 2: Reds-Brewers Tied, Cardinals Slip

x_posts.label: Post 6
x_posts.text:
NL Central standings after Week 2:

Reds: 8-4
Brewers: 8-4
Cardinals: 7-5
Pirates: 7-5
Cubs: 6-6

St. Louis lost 7-1 to Boston on Saturday. Chicago dropped two of three to Pittsburgh this weekend.

Five teams. Two games separating first and last. 👀 #NLCentral #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 4:00 PM ET*

Two weeks into the 2026 season, the NL Central looks nothing like anyone projected.

The Cincinnati Reds and Milwaukee Brewers are tied at 8-4 — both surprising teams with underrated starting pitching and timely offense. The St. Louis Cardinals and Pittsburgh Pirates are right behind them at 7-5. The Chicago Cubs trail at 6-6 after dropping two of three to Pittsburgh at Wrigley this weekend.

All five teams are within two games of first place. No team is running away with anything.

St. Louis took a hit Saturday, losing 7-1 to Boston. The Cardinals were expected to challenge for the division, but their pitching has been inconsistent and the lineup hasn't delivered in key moments. The Cubs have the talent to turn it around, but they need to start winning series at home.

The Brewers remain the most interesting story. Their offense with runners on base has been the best in MLB through two weeks. Milwaukee was projected around .500 by PECOTA entering the season. They're playing significantly above that.

The NL Central race in late September could feature five teams. That would be must-watch baseball.

Which NL Central team do you think finishes first?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with standings graphic*

NL Central after Week 2: Reds 8-4, Brewers 8-4, Cardinals 7-5, Pirates 7-5, Cubs 6-6. Two games separate first and last. The tightest division in baseball. 👀

x_posts.label: Post 7
x_posts.text:
Three MLB stats from the first 2 weeks that don't seem real:

• Dodgers: 45 runs in 4 road games — most since 1900
• Jeffrey Springs (OAK) no-hit the Yankees into the 7th
• Jose Altuve: 265 wRC+ in his first 27 plate appearances

It's April. Anything can happen. ⚾ #MLB

facebook.long_form:
*Posting Window: 8:00 PM ET*

Two weeks into the 2026 season, and baseball has already produced some numbers that don't quite compute.

The Los Angeles Dodgers scored 45 runs in their first four road games — the highest total in that span by any team since 1900. That's not a recent record. That's a 126-year record.

Jeffrey Springs of the Oakland Athletics carried a no-hitter against the New York Yankees into the seventh inning on April 9. At Yankee Stadium. The eventual hit broke the bid, and Springs ended up with a solid outing rather than history, but the moment was real.

Jose Altuve posted a 265 wRC+ through his first 27 plate appearances. That's not a stat — that's a declaration. For a player who faced questions about his longevity, he's answered with the most dominant early start of his career.

And then there's Matt Wallner of the Twins: a 47.7 percent strikeout rate in the first 10 games of his season. Historically, players striking out that often don't last long. Wallner hit three home runs anyway.

April baseball consistently produces numbers that would feel impossible in August. Small samples, cold weather, pitchers still finding their command. By June, regression will arrive for some of these names. But the first two weeks have been genuinely weird and genuinely fun.

What's been your favorite oddity from the first two weeks of the season?
facebook.image_caption:
*Post with stats graphic*

Three early-season MLB stats that don't seem real: Dodgers scored 45 runs in 4 road games (most since 1900). Jeffrey Springs no-hit the Yankees into the 7th. Altuve posted a 265 wRC+ in his first 27 PA. April baseball is wild. ⚾

research.facts:
Early-Season MLB Stat Oddities
**Story Type:** NEW STORY (fun stat compilation)
**Sources:**
- SI.com: "Dodgers 2026 dominance hasn't been seen in 126 years" — 45 road runs stat
- The Big Lead: "Athletics starting pitcher loses no-hitter in the 7th inning vs. Yankees" (April 9 Jeffrey Springs) — confirmed
- Sportsnaut: "Insane MLB stats entering April 7" — Altuve 265 wRC+, Wallner 47.7% K rate, Ben Rice 1.183 OPS
**Key Facts (confidence levels):**
- Dodgers scored 45 runs in first 4 road games — most since 1900 [HIGH — SI.com]
- Jeffrey Springs (Athletics) carried a no-hitter vs. Yankees through 6.2 innings on April 9 [HIGH — The Big Lead]
- Jose Altuve: 265 wRC+ in first 27 plate appearances [MEDIUM — Sportsnaut, single source]
- Matt Wallner (Twins): 47.7% strikeout rate but still hit 3 HRs in first 10 games [MEDIUM — Sportsnaut]
- Ben Rice (Yankees): 1.183 OPS, 100th percentile xwOBA through 11 games [MEDIUM — Sportsnaut]
**Freshness Verification:**
Stat compilation from first two weeks of season. Published April 12.
